_contract: Shared inline-span rendering conformance fixture (tesela-pfix.6) — the ONE
source of truth for how a block's prose text renders into a flat, ordered
list of inline spans, across web (parseInlineSpans in
web/src/lib/block-parser.ts, mirrored from the unfocused-block markdown pass
in web/src/lib/cm-decorations.ts's buildDecorations) and iOS
(BlockText.parseInlineSpans in
app/Tesela-iOS/Sources/Components/BlockText.swift). No Rust implementation
exists — Rust never renders UI — so this fixture has exactly two consumers,
not three; there is no 'Rust is the source of truth' rule here. Each engine
runs its REAL production span parser (the one BlockText / cm-decorations
actually render with) against every case. Web is the reference precedence
order (cm-decorations.ts is more capable and CodeMirror-coupled — atomic
ranges, marker-hide widgets, always-on vs. focus-gated passes — so this
fixture intentionally targets the smaller, portable RENDERING contract
block-parser.ts's parseInlineSpans captures, not those CodeMirror internals).
SCOPE — deliberately narrow to the flat, non-nesting inline surface both
platforms can realistically share:
* Covered kinds: bold (`**x**` / `__x__`), italic (`*x*`), inline code
(`` `x` ``), strikethrough (`~~x~~`), markdown link (`[x](url)`), and
wiki-link (`[[x]]`). All six are FLAT and MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE — a span's
inner text is never re-scanned for other markers (matches how both
engines implement this: one linear left-to-right scan, no recursion).
* Fixture text is always SINGLE-LINE prose (never spans a code fence or
contains '\n') — multi-line span-crossing is out of scope for both
engines' inline pass.
* `[[target|display]]` pipe-display wikilinks are OUT of scope — iOS's
existing (pre-tesela-pfix.6) wikilink handling keeps the bracket
interior verbatim (no pipe-splitting), so cases here never include a
`|` inside `[[...]]`. Fixing that is separate, un-scoped work.
* Block-level constructs (fenced code, images, ==highlight==, `>`
blockquote lines, `---` horizontal rules, `[!type]` callouts, ATX
headings) are OUT of scope — they are not inline spans in either
engine (fences are a pre-existing BLOCK-level segment in
BlockText.parseSegments, already at parity; the rest are TRIAGED in
the tesela-pfix.6 design notes as defer-or-do, not fixture-covered).
Case shape: { name, text, expected: [{ kind, text }] }
text     — raw single-line prose fed verbatim to the parser.
expected — the ordered, FLAT list of rendered spans. `kind: "plain"`
spans carry through unstyled runs (including any unterminated
marker, which is never treated as a span — see below).
`text` is always the DISPLAY text with delimiters stripped
(a link/wikilink span's `text` is its display text; the
URL/target is dropped — this is a rendering-only contract,
not a click-through target check).
kind     — one of: plain | bold | italic | code | strike | link | wikilink.
Precedence pinned by these cases (mirrors the priority order both
parseInlineSpans implementations must apply when multiple marker syntaxes
could start at the same character): code > bold > italic > strike >
wikilink > link. In practice these only compete via shared PREFIX
characters (`**` starts with `*`, so bold must be tried before italic);
the other marker syntaxes have disjoint leading characters so relative
order rarely matters, but is pinned anyway for determinism.
* Inline code content is fully literal — markers of any other kind
inside `` `...` `` never fire (the code match consumes them first).
* An unterminated marker (an opening delimiter with no matching close
on the same line) falls through as plain text, delimiter included.
* A lone unpaired `*` or a single `_` inside an identifier never forms
italic/bold — both require a real closing pair.

cases.name: mixed_paragraph_all_required_kinds
cases.text: Check **status**, run `build`, see [[Notes]], visit [site](https://x.com), and note ~~old~~ *new* info
